mnemonics: CHURCH-TURING THESIS: Any 'Computable Human Urge' Requires Church-Turing's Hypothesis, Therefore It's Sufficient. (Reminds that any algorithmically solvable problem is solvable by a TM)
DECIDABLE vs. RECOGNIZABLE: A Decider Always Halts; A Recognizer May Run Forever. (Highlights the key difference between the two language classes)
keyPoints: The Chomsky Hierarchy classifies languages (Regular, Context-Free, Context-Sensitive, Recursively Enumerable) and their corresponding automata (DFA/NFA, PDA, LBA, TM).
A language is Decidable (recursive) if a Turing Machine exists that halts and accepts every string in the language and halts and rejects every string not in it.
A language is Recognizable (recursively enumerable) if a Turing Machine exists that accepts every string in the language, but may loop forever on strings not in the language.
commonMistakes: Assuming all languages recognizable by a TM are also decidable. (Recognizable does not imply Decidable; the 'halting' requirement is the key difference).
Confusing the capabilities of different automata (e.g., thinking a PDA can solve problems requiring a TM, or that a DFA can handle non-regular languages).
